finding.contract_claim: Symbol `get_file_tools` is declared but appears unused — no references were found outside its own declaration.
finding.observed_behavior: No callers or importers of `get_file_tools` were found in the fixture project; consumer modules import the get_*_tool_definitions() variants instead, so this wrapper is never invoked even though the TOOL constant it returns is alive.
finding.triage_reasoning:
Comprehensive grep found `get_file_tools` only at its own declaration (tools.py:311). No caller, no import, no `__all__`, no `__init__.py` re-export. The only consumer of the underlying `SUBMIT_SHADOW_DOC_TOOL` constant is `shadow.py`, which imports and calls `get_file_tool_definitions()` — the newer `list[ToolDefinition]`-returning sibling defined at tools.py:405. The pattern repeats for all `get_*_tools()` (dict-returning) siblings (`get_directory_tools`, `get_match_doc_topics_tools`, `get_dead_code_tools`, `get_verify_doc_finding_tools`) — every consumer switched to the `*_tool_definitions()` variants, leaving the old wrappers as dead code.

Reality ✓ — the gap exists; the function is unreachable.
Significance ✓ — removing it shrinks the API surface and eliminates misleading stale wrappers.
Actionability ✓ — delete `get_file_tools()` (L311-313); optionally audit and delete the other stranded `get_*_tools()` siblings at the same time.
finding.suggested_fix: Delete `get_file_tools()` at tools.py L311-313. Also review and remove the other unreferenced `list[dict]`-returning siblings (`get_directory_tools`, `get_match_doc_topics_tools`, `get_dead_code_tools`, `get_verify_doc_finding_tools`) — all superseded by the `*_tool_definitions()` family.
